one_line_takeaway: Splitting purchase history with a fine-tuned BERT classifier and regenerating sensitive-item recommendations locally on Llama 3.2 1B recovers almost all recommendation utility of a full-data cloud LLM, but only partially protects privacy (~22% of sensitive items still leak) unless one falls back to a stricter, lower-utility categorical filter.
key_finding: The BERT Obfuscator + local Deobfuscator configuration nearly matches the no-privacy baseline on utility while cutting privacy leakage substantially, and beats the naive categorical-obfuscation approach on utility. Baseline (full history shared) gives Categorical HR@10 0.6263 / Semantic HR@10 0.3045 but 100% privacy leakage. BERT Obf + Deobf gives Categorical HR@10 0.6061 and Semantic HR@10 0.3117 (the latter actually slightly ABOVE baseline), with L1/L2 category distances of 0.3747/0.2267 (best alignment of all privacy-preserving configs) at PL_b = 22.28%, PL_s = 10.99%. Crucially, privacy is only PARTIAL: the BERT classifier misses ~22% of sensitive products (those it misses have low average sensitivity 0.2022 vs. overall mean 0.5422). The categorical obfuscator achieves 0% leakage but worse utility (Cat HR@10 0.4742 obf-only, 0.5258 with deobf). Deobfuscation recovers ~41% (L2)/43% (L1) of lost category alignment for the categorical route and ~15%/19% for the BERT route. BERT classifier itself: accuracy 0.911, F1 0.868, precision 0.815, recall 0.928. Overhead is edge-feasible: obfuscator peak GPU 471.89MB, deobfuscator peak GPU ~3.2GB; on a consumer laptop (RTX 3050 Ti 4GB, 8-bit Llama) obfuscation 0.18s, deobfuscation 6.38s, server rec 2.74s, adding ~3.82s end-to-end (deobf runs in parallel with the server call).
core_contribution: A hybrid, on-device privacy-preserving framework for LLM-based e-commerce product recommendation. The user's purchase history is split by an 'obfuscator' into sensitive vs. nonsensitive products; only nonsensitive products (plus the user query) are sent to a cloud LLM (ChatGPT-4o) to generate nonsensitive recommendations, while sensitive products are processed locally by a lightweight LLM (Llama 3.2 1B), the 'deobfuscator', to regenerate sensitive-category recommendations. The two lists are merged proportionally into a final top-n list. The paper's own novelty is (a) using a fine-tuned BERT context-aware classifier as the obfuscator (vs. a naive category-lookup 'Categorical Obfuscator' baseline they also build), and (b) the local deobfuscation step that recovers utility lost by removing sensitive items, without ever transmitting them to the server. Note: differential privacy, HaS, InferDPT, federated learning, homomorphic encryption/CipherGPT etc. appear only in Related Work as prior approaches the authors argue are unsuitable for text-based LLM recommendation; they are NOT this paper's method.
threat_model: A semi-honest ('honest-but-curious') cloud recommendation server that follows the protocol but may try to infer private user attributes (e.g., health status, illnesses) from the purchase history in the prompt. The server has no access to external data sources, cross-site tracking, or anything stored on the user's device, and processes only what is explicitly in each prompt; the paper assumes an idealized service that lets the user withhold specific purchases (unlike Amazon, where transactions are permanently logged). The user's own device is assumed trusted, secure, and not compromised by malware. The privacy asset is 'sensitive products' defined as items revealing health status or medical conditions (PII under HIPAA-style regimes); the goal is to keep those off the server while preserving recommendation utility.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) Privacy protection is incomplete for the flagship BERT configuration -- ~22% of sensitive products still leak due to classifier false negatives; the strong-privacy (0% leakage) option requires the lower-utility categorical obfuscator. (2) Sensitivity is a fixed, health-centric definition with no user-adaptive controls (marital status, income, gender not covered). (3) The obfuscator judges products individually and does NOT defend against cumulative/aggregate inference -- e.g., repeated purchases of individually-innocuous items (pain relievers, supplements) can still reveal chronic conditions. (4) Input/output length limits of the local and server LLMs force chunking for long histories, degrading context. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: sensitivity labels, sensitivity scores, and even the privacy-leakage weighting all derive from ChatGPT-4o judgments (circular / not human-validated); the threat model assumes an idealized server that lets users withhold purchases and does no cross-request linkage, which real platforms violate; and evaluation relies on proxy HR@10 metrics (category/semantic) rather than true next-item hits because exact-match HR@10 was near zero on this data.

datasets_used: 2023 Amazon Reviews dataset (McAuley Lab), 10 categories; sensitive = Health & Personal Care, Health & Household, Beauty & Personal Care
10,000 products sampled from sensitive categories for BERT training, labeled sensitive/nonsensitive by ChatGPT-4o (70/20/10 stratified split)
User histories: users with >=30 purchases, last 20 purchases each
quantitative_results: Baseline (full history): Categorical HR@10 0.6263, Semantic HR@10 0.3045, PL_b 100%, PL_s 100%
BERT Obf + Deobf: Categorical HR@10 0.6061, Semantic HR@10 0.3117, L1 0.3747, L2 0.2267, PL_b 22.2833%, PL_s 10.9899%
BERT Obf Only: Categorical HR@10 0.5960, Semantic HR@10 0.2866, L1 0.4610, L2 0.2672, PL_b 22.2833%, PL_s 10.9899%
Categorical Obf + Deobf: Categorical HR@10 0.5258, Semantic HR@10 0.2994, L1 0.4773, L2 0.2847, PL_b 0%, PL_s 0%
Categorical Obf Only: Categorical HR@10 0.4742, Semantic HR@10 0.2444, L1 0.8462, L2 0.4860, PL_b 0%
Only Local (Llama only): Categorical HR@10 0.4667, Semantic HR@10 0.2398, L1 0.7711, L2 0.4538, PL_b 0%
BERT classifier: accuracy 0.911, F1 0.868, precision 0.815, recall 0.928, eval loss 0.181
Missed sensitive products have avg sensitivity 0.2022 vs overall mean 0.5422 (std 0.2317)
Utility recovery from deobfuscation: Categorical 41.42% (L2)/43.59% (L1); BERT 15.16% (L2)/18.72% (L1)
Overhead: Obfuscator peak GPU 471.89MB alloc / 601.55MB reserved, 3.69 cores; Deobfuscator peak GPU 3044.50MB alloc / 3225.77MB reserved, 5.61 cores
Consumer laptop (RTX 3050 Ti 4GB, 8-bit Llama) latency: obfuscation 0.1808s, deobfuscation 6.3816s, server rec 2.7428s, total added ~3.8196s
baselines_compared: Baseline R (full history sent to cloud, no privacy) -- utility upper bound
Only Local (Llama 3.2 1B only, all processing on-device)
Categorical Obfuscator Only (R') -- fixed sensitive-category removal
BERT Obfuscator + Deobfuscator (R'') -- the proposed method
